Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Why do you always lose money on Polymarket?
data.description:
Do you know why you can't beat the leaders on Polymarket? Because they scrutinize the rules, nitpicking like lawyers reviewing contracts. In April 2026, a controversy on Polymarket regarding the Venezuelan leader caused a stir in the community. One market on Polymarket asked, "Who will be the leader of Venezuela at the end of 2026?" Many traders instinctively thought: Maduro is in a US prison, Delcy is running the cabinet in Caracas, and Delcy is clearly the one in power, so they bet on Delcy. However, the rules and supplementary explanations clearly state: "officially holds" refers to someone officially appointed and sworn in. The UN-recognized Venezuelan government has not formally removed or replaced Maduro, and official government information still identifies him as president. The rules also specifically add a clause: "A temporary authorization to exercise presidential powers does not equate to a transfer of the presidency." According to these rules, even if Maduro remains in a US prison, he is still the legitimate president of Venezuela. There are many similar examples: After Polymarket issued its stablecoin, the market debated "What is the FDV of the Polymarket token?": Is a stablecoin a token? A difference of just one word. Iranian uranium: The standard of "agreement," conditional statements vs. formal signing of an agreement. These cases share the same logic: at Polymarket, the rules are the core. However, when disputes arise regarding the rules, Polymarket has a complete adjudication process to resolve them. This article will introduce how this mechanism works and where it is similar to, and where there are fundamental differences between, and traditional courts. I. Polymarket's Adjudication Mechanism Ambiguity in the rule text not only causes pricing disagreements, but it can also escalate into formal disputes during settlement. Polymarket handles a large number of market settlements daily, with markets involving political statements, diplomatic pronouncements, and military actions being particularly prone to disputes. Disputes are actually a normal part of the market. Ambiguity creates pricing disagreements during the trading phase and escalates into disputes during the settlement phase; it's the same issue manifesting differently at two points in time. To resolve these disputes, Polymarket has established a comprehensive adjudication process, with two paths: normal settlement and dispute adjudication. Step 1: Submit a Propose When the market meets the settlement conditions, anyone can submit a resolution, stating whether the market should rule YES or NO. Submitting a proposal requires a 750 USDC deposit as collateral. This deposit serves as the submitter's endorsement of their judgment. If the market has no objections, the user who submitted the proposal will receive a 5 USDC reward. Currently, only 1782 users have submitted proposals in the market, and the most profitable user has accumulated $281,000.

Step Two: 2-Hour Challenge Window (Dispute)

After submitting the proposal, a 2-hour challenge period begins. This is the first fork in the entire process.

If no one raises an objection within 2 hours, the system assumes the proposal is correct, the market settles directly, and the process ends. Most markets follow this path.

If someone believes the proposal result is incorrect, they can submit a challenge within these 2 hours, which also requires staking 750 USDC. A successful challenge earns a bonus of 250 USDC.

Few users specialize in Dispute mode. The user who earned the most in the Dispute stage was 0xB7A, with a profit of $17,123. Step 3: A Discussion Period of Up to 48 Hours After entering the disputed phase, both sides proceed to the UMA Discord discussion stage. The purpose of this stage is to allow each party to submit arguments and evidence: interpretations of the rule text, relevant news reports, historical precedents, official statements—any material that can support their position can be presented at this stage. The discussion period lasts up to 48 hours and is the only stage in the entire process where reasons can be fully presented. The quality of this stage largely determines the direction of the subsequent vote. Step 4: 48-Hour Voting After the discussion, the voting phase for UMA token holders begins, divided into two 24-hour phases. The first phase is blind voting. Each voter must make an independent judgment based on their own understanding of the rules, rather than following the lead of large holders. The second phase is public voting. Votes not made public during this phase are considered abstentions and are invalidated. After the voting concludes, UMA sets two settlement thresholds that must be met simultaneously to complete the decision: Regarding participation: at least 5 million tokens are required to participate in the vote, ensuring sufficient representativeness of the decision. Regarding absolute consensus: the winning party must receive more than 65% of the votes, not simply a 51% majority. If both thresholds are not met simultaneously, the auction fails, and a re-vote is initiated, up to a maximum of four times. If no consensus is reached after four rounds, Polymarket has the right to intervene directly in the adjudication. Step 5: Automatic Settlement After the voting results are confirmed, the market automatically settles the accounts, and funds are distributed according to the results. There is no appeal, no retrial, and no opportunity for remedy. The entire dispute resolution process, from challenge submission to final settlement, typically takes less than a week.

II. Polymarket and Traditional Courts: Same Logic, Different Design

On the surface, the adjudication process in a polymarket is highly similar to that of a traditional court: both have a party making a claim, a party challenging the claim, a discussion and presentation phase, and finally, an adjudicator giving a result.

However, the two systems are fundamentally different in one design: the separation of powers.

1⃣The power of the court is isolated

In traditional courts, plaintiffs and defendants only have the right to present their cases, not the right to adjudicate. Judges only have the right to adjudicate, not the right to hold a vested interest. More importantly, judges and cases must remain independent. Once a judge has any conflict of interest with a case, they must recuse themselves and the case must be heard by another judge.

The adjudicator and the interested party are never the same person.

2⃣Polymarket lacks this isolation

UMA token holders are the adjudicators, but they can also hold positions in the controversial market. The direction of the adjudication directly affects their profits and losses.

The fact that the referee and the interested party are the same person is called a conflict of interest in traditional courts, which would be subject to mandatory recusal. However, in Polymarket, it's legal and normal. This design flaw is the root of the following two problems: 1. Why does the discussion phase fail? In court, the plaintiff's and defendant's positions are fixed from the moment the lawsuit is filed. Lawyers won't switch sides mid-trial, nor will they withdraw their statements simply because the opposing side is aggressive. Clear positions and defined roles form the basis of the entire debate. UMA Discord's discussions face two problems simultaneously. Herd mentality: Discussions are conducted openly and anonymously. Once an influential KOL expresses their opinion, it easily becomes a bandwagon effect. Many participants simply post a "P1" or "P2" without providing any reason.

Shift in Stance: Participants in the discussion simultaneously hold positions in the controversial market. When their positions change, their stance naturally shifts. This is why it's common to see opinions posted and then deleted on UMA Discord.

The root of both problems is the same: the arbitrator and the interested party are not separated. Courts use mandatory recusal to separate these two roles, ensuring the stability of stances during discussions. Polymarkets lack this separation.

2. Why are the rulings not transparent?

In court, the judge hears complete statements from both sides before making a ruling. The ruling specifies which side's arguments were adopted, the basis for the ruling, and the reasons for the ruling. The losing party may be dissatisfied, but at least they know where they went wrong and can strengthen their arguments next time.

These judgments form a system of precedents that can be studied, and subsequent judges, lawyers, and parties can cite them, thus making the standards of judgment verifiable, learnable, and predictable. After the UMA vote, there is only one result: YES or NO. The parties involved in the discussion do not know what the voters read, believed, or why they favored one side. If they win, they don't know which argument played a role; if they lose, they don't know where their persuasiveness was lacking. Because the logic of the judgments is never made public, the results of the disputes are difficult to learn and accumulate. The court's judgments form a system of precedents, and Polymarket's rulings leave only one result. III. In Conclusion Therefore, Polymarket is never simply a market for "guessing events correctly"; it is more like a system that translates real-world events into legal texts and then translates those legal texts into settlement results. Understanding the rules is just as important as conducting research. A leader's advantage often stems from a deep understanding of the rules—knowing what the system recognizes and what decisions it makes. Whoever recognizes the gap between "reality" and "rules" earlier has a greater chance of profiting from the price discrepancies created by misinterpretations, controversies, and emotions.

data.title: The Real-World Stage of the Web4.0 Race in 2026
data.description:
Since 2026, a concept deeply related to AI has frequently appeared in crypto research and investment institutions: Web4.0. Unlike previous popular narratives, this discussion focuses not on new consensus mechanisms or higher throughput, but on the shift in the implementing entity—from humans operating the internet to autonomous AI agents directly participating in economic activities. GoToMars Research Institute has been continuously tracking the direction of the integration of AI and cryptography. This report starts with the actual progress of infrastructure construction, assessing the actual development stage, structural opportunities, and practical constraints of this sector in April 2026. I. The Long-Overlooked Structural Mismatch Before discussing Agent Economics, we need to understand a real-world problem: current AI models possess considerable perception and decision-making capabilities, but they almost entirely lack the power to act at the commercial level. Every link in the traditional financial system, from account opening and identity verification to payment authorization and contract signing, is based on the real-name registration of natural or legal persons. An AI program running in the cloud cannot legally open commercial bank accounts, nor can it independently sign legally binding agreements. As a result, AI can only assist humans as a tool and cannot assume the role of an independent economic entity. A deeper mismatch appears at the granularity of payments. Taking Stripe's standard fee rate as an example, a single transaction incurs a fixed cost of 2.9% plus $0.30. This pricing assumes a scenario of relatively large, infrequent human consumption. However, the actual behavior of agents is completely the opposite; a single API call may only cost $0.001 to $0.01, but the call frequency is as high as dozens of times per second. Under these transaction characteristics, the fixed fees of traditional card organizations already exceed the transaction amount, making it impossible to support commercial micro-payment scenarios. The table below summarizes the differences between the two types of users in several key dimensions. Table 1. Characteristic Differences Between Human Users and AI Agents in Key Interaction Dimensions. This mismatch means that even with powerful models, AI can only play the role of information processor within the existing financial system. The prerequisite for truly making agents economic agents is finding a set of underlying protocols that transcend the traditional financial identity framework.

II. Why Crypto Infrastructure Has Become an Alternative Path

Permissionless blockchain networks provide a structural solution to the above problems. In networks such as Ethereum, Solana, or TRON, generating an on-chain address only requires local code to generate a public-private key pair once, a process completed in milliseconds, without any centralized approval. This means that AI agents can create independent identity carriers for themselves or their derived subtasks at any time and directly participate in on-chain transactions as asset recipients. The introduction of stablecoins further solves the value unit problem. USDT and USDC currently have a combined circulation of over $250 billion, providing a stable pricing and settlement medium. In addition, with smart contracts and composable DeFi protocols, complex financial operations such as fund allocation, lending, and hedging can be completed through programmatic interfaces, no longer restricted by business hours and manual approval.

III. Progress and Constraints of Three Key Protocols

For an Agent to truly become an economic entity, it must fill three protocol gaps on top of the existing blockchain infrastructure: payment, identity, and tool invocation.

Over the past twelve months, these three gaps have been filled by three representative agreements, all of which made significant progress between the second half of 2025 and the first quarter of 2026. Figure 2: Timeline of Key Milestones in Web4.0 Infrastructure Development (May 2025 to April 2026) The payment layer is filled by the x402 protocol released by Coinbase in May 2025, which reuses the long-dormant 402 Payment Required protocol in the HTTP protocol. Status codes allow the server to directly return a payment request in a single HTTP request, and the client can obtain resources after completing the payment via stablecoin signature. On April 2, 2026, the x402 Foundation officially joined the Linux Foundation to conduct open-source governance. This foundation was jointly initiated by Coinbase, Cloudflare, and Stripe, with over 20 organizations participating in governance, including AWS, American Express, Google, Mastercard, Microsoft, Polygon Labs, Shopify, Solana Foundation, and Visa. The identity layer is filled by ERC-8004, led by the Ethereum ecosystem. This standard was jointly drafted by representatives from MetaMask, the Ethereum Foundation, Google, and Coinbase, and was deployed on the Ethereum mainnet reference registry on January 29, 2026. The tool invocation layer is led by the MCP protocol, open-sourced by Anthropic in November 2024, and was donated to the newly established Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation on December 9, 2025. It is worth noting that the progress of the protocol layer is significantly faster than the accumulation of actual commercial traffic. According to a report by Unchained on April 3, 2026, x402 has an average of approximately 55,000 transactions per day on the Base network, and has processed approximately 97 million transactions on the Base chain to date. Data from on-chain analytics firm Artemis shows that the x402 cross-chain transaction volume averages approximately $28,000 per day, with an average transaction value of about $0.20. Meanwhile, about half of the current transactions are estimated to be fraudulent or testing activities, indicating that genuine commercial traffic is still in its very early stages. Therefore, the time lag between protocol standardization and commercial adoption is the most critical observation point at this stage. Table 2: Progress and Real-World Constraints of Key Three-Layer Protocols in Web4.0 (Image source: Publicly disclosed by Linux Foundation, Ethereum Foundation, Anthropic, The Block, and Unchained; compiled by GoToMars Research Institute. Data as of mid-April 2026.) IV. Typical Case: Computing Power Layer – The First Basic Asset to Be Positively Recognized by the Capital Market While payment protocols and application scenarios are still in the early stages of commercial validation, the computing power assets at the very bottom of the Web4.0 stack have been the first to successfully connect with traditional financial channels. This is an easily overlooked but highly significant phenomenon – traditional capital did not wait for Agent applications to be successfully implemented before entering the market, but instead chose to establish asset exposure from the computing power layer first. The most typical example comes from Grayscale. On December 30, 2025, Grayscale filed a Form S-1 registration statement with the SEC for the conversion of its Bittensor Trust into a spot ETP, intending to list it on the NYSE Arca under the ticker symbol GTAO. On the same day, Bitwise also filed eleven registration applications (Form N-1A) with the SEC for strategic crypto ETFs, including a product specifically tracking TAO—the Bitwise TAO Strategy ETF. On April 2, 2026, Grayscale further filed Amendment No. 1 with the SEC, pushing the product closer to its official listing. In Switzerland, the Safello Bittensor Staked TAO ETP (ticker symbol: STAO), jointly issued by Deutsche Digital Assets and Safello, was listed on the SIX Swiss Exchange on November 19, 2025, trading in USD, 100% physically backed, and offering staking rewards. This means that before North American ETFs have been finally approved, the European market has already introduced TAO-related products to mainstream exchanges. Institutional secondary asset allocation is also accelerating. On April 7, 2026, Grayscale rebalanced its Grayscale Decentralized AI Fund, increasing TAO's weight from 31.35% to 43.06%, making it the largest single asset weight change in this adjustment, and no other assets were added or removed during this rebalancing. This weight change indicates that institutions are beginning to truly incorporate decentralized AI into the main AI narrative—in the past, institutional expression of this theme was almost entirely done through Nvidia and a few large model vendors; TAO is the first crypto-native asset to be significantly re-allocated by mainstream decentralized AI-themed funds. The scarcity on the supply side further reinforces the long-term logic of this trend. Bittensor completed its first halving on December 14, 2025, reducing the daily new supply from 7,200 to 3,600, maintaining the same supply rhythm as Bitcoin (Grayscale Research, December 2025; Bittensor official documentation). With approximately 70% of the circulating supply being long-term pledged and the limited surplus being absorbed by listed company treasuries and ETP structures, the circulation pressure on computing power layer assets has structurally tightened. This is the fundamental reason why institutions are willing to establish positions before the application layer. However, a real risk must also be pointed out here. On April 10, 2026, Covenant AI, the operator of the leading subnet of the Bittensor network, announced its withdrawal from the network and sold approximately 37,000 TAO tokens, triggering a price correction of over 20% and reigniting concerns about the centralized nature of network governance. This event serves as a reminder to institutional investors that while the logic of assetizing the computing power layer is valid, it is still in a period of adjustment regarding both governance structure and network stability, and cannot be simply compared to Bitcoin's early institutional adoption path. The signals released by the computing power layer have two layers of meaning. First, the opening of institutional channels means that node operators, staking service providers, subnet developers, and compliant custodians built around this layer will receive structural dividends when funds enter, which is the direction in which they can participate deeply in the next stage. Second, after institutions bind TAO and decentralized AI as configurable assets, the valuation anchor for the remaining layers of the entire Web4.0 stack will be gradually established, and the assetization paths of the payment, identity, and application layers will also have a reference coordinate. V. Several Judgments from GoToMars Research Institute Based on the above observations, the Web4.0 track is currently in a stage of concentrated infrastructure construction and relatively lagging commercial adoption. The three forces of narrative, capital, and protocol have completed their initial convergence, but real on-chain commercial traffic is still in the early stages of verification. For investment institutions and project owners, this stage presents both opportunities and risks. The key lies in accurately judging the misalignment between the agreement window and the commercial window. Table 4: GoToMars Research Institute's Three Judgments on the Web4.0 Sector. Source: GoToMars Research Institute, April 2026. The first point is that the outcome of the standard game will be clear within the next twelve months. While x402 and ERC-8004 have achieved some breakthroughs, the formation of real traffic will still take time. Once the standard is established, toolchains, SDKs, and auditing services built around it will see concentrated opportunities. The second point concerns the migration of product forms. Product architectures that can be assembled and programmatically invoked, represented by Skills, will gradually be replicated from AI Trading to traditional industries such as insurance actuarial science, supply chain fulfillment, and content copyright settlement. This direction is a vertical incubation opportunity worth focusing on in the next stage. The third point is that competition in stablecoin clearing layers will become a key venue for the actual implementation of payment layers. TRON, Solana, Base, and BNB Chain are all vying for mindshare in this layer. Projects that build comprehensive Agent financial operating systems around different stablecoin networks have strong structural opportunities. The window of opportunity for securing a foothold in the underlying infrastructure is narrowing, while the vertical application layer, capable of transforming protocol capabilities into real-world business loops, presents a more noteworthy opportunity between 2026 and 2027. The completeness of the protocol layer determines the potential boundaries of upper-layer applications, but the true realization of value ultimately hinges on the service capabilities provided to machine users in specific scenarios.

data.title: After 300 million, another 300 million—do people still dare to deposit money in the DeFi space?
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

April was a turbulent month for the crypto industry. Shortly after Drift, the leading DEX in the Solana ecosystem Perp, suffered a "Friday Night Massacre" and lost $285 million, the market entered a period of extreme volatility with the "bungee jumping" of RAVE.

Just as RAVE's popularity was beginning to cool down, the DeFi market was dealt another blow by the hacking of KelpDAO, a leading Ethereum LRT (liquidity restaking) protocol.

And just as RAVE's popularity was beginning to subside, the DeFi market was hit hard by the hacking of KelpDAO, a leading Ethereum LRT (liquidity restaking) protocol.

On April 18th, KelpDAO was compromised by hackers exploiting a vulnerability in its LayerZero-based cross-chain bridge. Approximately 116,500 rsETH were illegally withdrawn, resulting in a loss of $292 million. This theft was even larger than the Drift attack, making it the largest on-chain security incident of 2026 to date. The hackers did not breach the mainnet staking contract, nor did they leak private keys; it was merely a tiny crack in cross-chain verification that triggered a combination of risks in DeFi. When re-staking leverage is combined with the ambition of multi-chain expansion, after three years of sprinting down the path of "yield priority," DeFi once again faces the existential question of "yield supremacy" versus "security supremacy." A single point of verification vulnerability triggers the LRT crisis, with KelpDAO suffering a nearly $300 million theft. KelpDAO, the protagonist of the theft, was once a star player in the LRT field. Its business logic precisely addresses market pain points, creating a "three-in-one" model. Users can encapsulate LST (liquidity staking) assets such as stETH and rETH into rsETH, retaining the basic returns from ETH staking while also receiving additional restaking rewards from EigenLayer. Furthermore, they can use rsETH across various DeFi lending and mining scenarios. To seize market share, KelpDAO aggressively expanded to 16 public chains. With its high yield and high liquidity, rsETH has become a mainstream collateral asset for various Layer 2 and Aave blockchains, deeply embedded in the Ethereum DeFi ecosystem. This multi-chain architecture heavily relies on the underlying cross-chain communication protocol provided by LayerZero, which became the epicenter of the disaster. On April 20th, LayerZero released a recap of the incident, stating that KelpDAO was attacked, resulting in a loss of approximately $290 million. Initial indications suggest that the attack may have been carried out by a highly sophisticated state actor, most likely North Korea's Lazarus Group, more specifically TraderTraitor. Because KelpDAO uses a single-signature configuration, this incident was limited to its rsETH configuration and did not affect any other cross-chain assets or applications. Meanwhile, LayerZero acknowledged that KelpDAO was using only a 1/1 DVN configuration, posing a "single point of failure," and is contacting all applications using a 1/1 DVN configuration to migrate to a multi-signature setup with redundancy. However, LayerZero's failure to previously urge KelpDAO to make changes or enforce multi-signature configuration also makes it partly to blame. Hackers specifically targeted LayerZero's downstream infrastructure, compromising two independent nodes and causing DVN to confirm transactions that never occurred. According to LayerZero, hackers obtained the list of RPCs used by LayerZero Labs DVN, compromised two independent nodes, replaced the op-geth binary, and launched a DDoS attack on the uninfected RPCs, triggering a failover that allowed DVN to confirm transactions that never occurred. In short, the hackers "creatively" activated rsETH withdrawal permissions. Even more frightening is that if the emergency blacklist mechanism hadn't been triggered in the last three minutes, the hackers would have taken an additional $100 million, bringing the total loss to over $400 million. This collapse was foreshadowed. The hackers' attack path directly targeted a common industry weakness: the vulnerability of the protocol verification mechanism. In its fervent pursuit of cross-chain efficiency, KelpDAO ignored its long-standing single point of verification problem, ultimately becoming the hackers' entry point. This isn't the first time KelpDAO has exposed security vulnerabilities. Last May, due to a unit scaling error during a contract upgrade, the protocol minted 31.2 quintillions (five quadrillion) of rsETH. Although these were promptly destroyed to avoid loss, they already exposed its security vulnerabilities. The intense competition in the restaking market has made security a casualty. To continuously expand its scale, KelpDAO constantly integrates new LST assets and expands its L2 network. However, with each additional chain and asset, the attack surface expands exponentially. A seasoned DeFi player points out that the customer acquisition cost of L2 TVL is expected to increase further, with a large amount of TVL flowing back to L1. The "double-edged sword" of multi-chain expansion ultimately becomes a sharp blade piercing the protocol itself and the entire DeFi ecosystem. Aave was poisoned by rsETH, triggering a $200 million bad debt and a $6.6 billion capital flight. DeFi is like Lego bricks; if one piece breaks, the whole thing collapses. After obtaining the illicit rsETH, the hackers didn't immediately dump it on DEXs. Instead, they employed an "asset poisoning" strategy: depositing rsETH as "high-quality collateral" into Aave to extract real, highly liquid assets. Aave V3/V4 accepts rsETH as eligible collateral on Ethereum and Arbitrum. The hackers deposited rsETH and borrowed large amounts of WETH, USDC, and USDT, converting the illicit assets into bad debt for the protocol. According to Chaos Labs' estimates, the scale of bad debt facing Aave far exceeds market expectations, approaching 2... $100 million. Following the bad debt news, AAVE tokens quickly fell by about 18%. Since the end of last year, Aave seems to have been experiencing a severe downturn. After experiencing governance chaos and a wave of service provider exodus, it has now become a prime liquidity outlet for hackers due to its integration with the rsETH market. The leaked on-chain data further fueled the fire for Aave. Justin Sun was detected to have urgently redeemed 53,665 ETH from Aave, worth $126 million. His withdrawal is seen as a bellwether of whales losing confidence in the protocol's security. This was followed by a market-wide capital flight. DeFiLlama data shows that Aave recorded a net outflow of $6.6 billion in a single day, a sharp decrease of 23% in its fund size. While the root cause wasn't Aave, this incident serves as a profound test of its risk management mechanisms. Some users pointed out that community members publicly warned of KelpDAO's single point of verification risk on the Aave governance forum as early as 15 months ago. However, the Aave team has not proposed any solutions. In contrast, Spark delisted rsETH in January of this year. DeFi researcher CM bluntly stated: The entire Sky system adopts a proactive and tightening risk control philosophy, which may lead to slower protocol development, but it has demonstrated its value at critical moments. Justin Sun's 53,600 ETH withdrawal was also deposited into Spark. Within two days, the Spark token surged by more than 50%, a stark contrast to AAVE. Todd, co-founder of Nothing Research, believes that facing nearly $200 million in bad debts, Aave may activate its "Umbrella" insurance module. While the Umbrella module provides the first line of defense, its liquidity pool size is clearly insufficient to fully cover the approximately $200 million in asset losses. In the short term, Aave's self-rescue efforts only delay the crisis, not resolve it properly. The main shortfall still needs to be filled through Aave's protocol profitability or token issuance; specific solutions will be left for further discussion within the community. Isolation pools + mandatory insurance + risk repricing: security is no longer a "free lunch." The KelpDAO incident signals the official end of the LRT craze, and the DeFi market will usher in three irreversible risk control reforms. The segregation of the lending market: Aave's non-segregated lending model is becoming history, with assets confined to completely independent "siloed pools." Even if a single asset encounters a problem, it will not affect assets in other liquidity pools. Curve founder Michael Egorov points out that while non-segregated lending models offer good scalability, they are riskier, and he recommends a fully segregated or hybrid model. While a fully segregated architecture may reduce capital efficiency, it will significantly enhance the system's resilience. Mandatory Insurance Module: The Umbrella module will drive protocol insurance from an "optional configuration" to a "mandatory component." In the future, any new asset seeking to list on mainstream lending platforms like Aave may be required to deposit a certain percentage of collateral into the corresponding insurance vault as the primary source of compensation in the event of default or theft in the relevant market. Risk Repricing of DeFi Assets: OneKey founder Yishi bluntly stated that the returns and risks of DeFi are currently completely disproportionate, and security has rigid costs. The market will re-price risks. Protocol fees and infrastructure costs will face upward pressure; otherwise, they cannot support security investments. Therefore, DeFi assets need to be repriced based on their underlying security. The risks of encapsulated assets such as LRT are significantly higher than those of native assets, and lending platforms should incorporate the risk discount of encapsulated assets into their risk control models. The KelpDAO hack serves as a stark mirror, reflecting the collective disregard for security in the pursuit of maximum returns and multi-chain expansion within the DeFi sector. The nearly $300 million loss is costly, but if it prompts DeFi to shift from blindly pursuing composition to prioritizing stability, then this may be the necessary tuition fee for the industry to mature. In the aftermath of the KelpDAO incident, the market is gradually realizing that the true value of DeFi lies in providing a more transparent, secure, and resilient financial infrastructure. When the tsunami recedes, what remains will be an even stronger foundation.

data.title: Harness Arbitrage Period: Saving DeFi from the Edge of SaaS
data.description:
Looking back over 500 years, the labor-capital conflict under capitalism has always been marked by the continuous victory of capital. On the production side, the participation of labor has gradually shrunk to the level of operating machines; on the consumption side, user value lies in producing and using data for the platform. The combined effect of these two factors supports the valuation of companies in the capital market. However, the organizational model of people has long been unable to be fully quantified. White-collar KPIs/OKRs remain a form of hierarchy, and annual salaries of millions and piece-rate wages are variations of Taylorism. Without a clear formula, capital cannot value them, thus affecting capital efficiency. Whether algorithmic stablecoins are the holy grail of DeFi remains to be seen, but the computability of organizations is indeed a measuring cup for financial leverage. The large-scale model's decision to brute-force attack using tokens is merely a symptom of the collapse of security SaaS; product design is underway, and the key lies in scaling up and replacing niche professional capabilities. Innovation is venturing into uncharted territory. This offers us endless insights, especially given the current collapse of DeFi's DAO model and the increasing bankruptcy of token economics. In short, why are AI's organizational and token models more efficient than DeFi? How did all this begin? Token devaluation and agent practicality. For a 300% profit, capitalists can sell their noose; to keep their jobs, workers can write skills for agents. At the capital level, agents empowered by skills possess a sacred status equal to profits. Agents represent the refinement of "human abilities" into skills; moreover, human organizations become interactive ritual chains centered around agents. From Prompt and Context to the current Harness Project, all are turning human organizational models into uncharted territory, or at least reducing the number of people. Your next colleague won't be a robot, but could be an instinctive "ability." This isn't a fantasy; the data-driven scaling law is gradually becoming ineffective. However, data collection and production are no longer crucial. Before AGI succeeds, new valuation targets are needed. Image caption: Content is no longer valuable. Comprehensive information: @ARKInvest. From Claude's initial foray into programming to implement AGI, AI has transcended the entertainment model of chat windows, penetrating existing markets in real-world fields such as programming, security, and the recently released design. Will this disruptive innovation ultimately create new economic growth, or will it drag the economy into a permanent low-employment model where tokens are used for jobs and people are laid off? We are witnessing this process. However, the current cheapening of tokens, which decentralizes capabilities previously monopolized by a few large enterprises to micro and small enterprises, thereby creating super-individuals, is not a fantasy. Taking China as an example, token usage has increased from 100 billion/day in 2024 to 100 trillion/day by the end of 2025 to 140 trillion/day now, and content and data production is about to enter a zero-cost era. It is important to note that the scarcity of computing power is a relative state. Large enterprises no longer monopolize "capabilities," but still want to maintain their existing advantages by monopolizing "computing power," but they cannot stop the inevitable trend of overall token cheapening. While there are various paradigm comparisons of large-scale foundational models, the evolutionary process of "how AI helps humans" has long been overlooked. In my view, Harness is a spatial model that allows agents to focus on tasks within boundaries for the first time, employing a depth-first strategy, unlike the breadth-first approach of question-answering algorithms. Image caption: Agent Evolution History Image source: @zuoyeweb3 From the moment the Tab key was first used for code completion, it was only a matter of time before humans became the input layer for AI. The cost of trial and error is reduced exponentially, allowing for more interesting experiments with human collaboration models: Software: SaaS, where human capabilities no longer originate from humans, but from the emergence of agents. Hardware: Computing power cards + HBM, data centers directly serve AI needs for the first time. Space: Harness, not a physical space for human collaboration, but a digital space for agent interaction. Interaction: With the demise of the Doubao phone, Google supports GUI agents at the Android system level. AI's ability to say things doesn't have strong commercial value; the cost of text generation is low for humans. However, "what to do" will cause token consumption to surpass that of image and video generation, similar to AWS selling usage time, not servers. AI isn't selling tokens, but rather "work capabilities." This is the root of the SaaS industry's fears. Unfortunately, DeFi has become SaaS, not a large-scale model. The SaaSification of DeFi Protocols. DeFi isn't outdated, but it's prematurely matured. AI is reinventing software engineering; it's not just SaaS that's being replaced, but SaaS is undoubtedly the most typical example. Even with Bloomberg Terminal, its most important commercial value isn't technological advancement, but the authority of its information. This authority is built upon decades of industry connections, networks, and other non-standard data. Agents offer an option: to predict the future from data. Even a risky next step might surpass competitors and earn a small profit.

Image description: SaaS crashing

Image source: @zuoyeweb3

You can understand it as the Agent cleverly taking advantage of the profit-seeking nature of capital. Of course, it can wait for complete Bloomberg terminal information, or it can use pieced-together, inaccurate data to gamble on profits.

This isn't new. IBKR founder Thomas Peterfee was the first to "invent," or rather assemble, a physical trading terminal in the financial sector, and it all started with an idle P101. If a way of utilizing data can generate more profit, then you can get more data, and the flywheel starts. SaaS monopolized the past; AI sells the future. Unfortunately, we'll be entering DeFi from here. Remember the API paywalls of Dune/DeFiLlama, begging for data, or the eventual closure of Arkham Exchange? Data in the crypto industry has never been valuable. But the crypto industry is a direct and open financial system, and the data it generates can be repeatedly learned, even in AI... Previously, the speed of forked projects had been reduced to months, and PumpFun's clone, Meme, could be compressed to seconds. Here lies a counterintuitive conclusion: DeFi is a testbed for the financial system, and the AI+DeFi we're experimenting with today will become the template for future financial evolution. For example, before the 2008 financial crisis, unsecured LIBOR triggered a financial tsunami, which was later replaced by SOFR, an indicator generated by US Treasury bond trading. However, the over-collateralization mechanism ensured the finality of DeFi liquidation. For example, large-scale model producers don't want to sell tokens based on consumption; they insist on tiered marketing, customized capabilities, and professional modifications. Token economics has twisted the concept of "use value" into a convoluted mess. Crypto Token Focusing on use value, AI Token focuses on economic value. From this perspective, DeFi hacks are merely a routine stress test, an external entropy that prevents open systems from patching bugs on their own. Similar to the dark humor of Catch-22, without external signaling, encryption assumes the current environment is secure; however, in the event of a security crisis, it collapses into a centralized processing system. For example, in the Drift incident, the blame was shifted to the slow-freezing Circle. Image caption: Code cannot solve security problems. Image source: @zuoyeweb3 It can be said that before the leap in AI capabilities, DeFi had already completed its SaaS transformation, only able to charge based on the number of transactions, unable to directly migrate "finance" onto the blockchain. RWA's lack of liquidity on-chain is a problem DeFi doesn't have a good solution for. However, the evolution of Agent capabilities seems to offer a glimmer of hope for rewriting the rules of DeFi, though the exact details are still unclear. Token economics: Distribution of usage across channels, allocation based on "capital efficiency"; Rule setting: Mythos provides security and finality, AI defenses against zero-day crises; Human organization: Great, DeFi has long been a few people managing billions. The Revival of Engineering Narratives Where does security come from? The determinism of Turing machines. Where does danger come from? Infinite possibilities. YC Garry Tan's saying, "Fat Skill, Thin Harness," resonates deeply with me. Essentially, it's about establishing fundamental rules—a kind of "freedom based on order." Turing machines can be infinitely combined, the von Neumann architecture always has a time lag in its in-memory computation, and large models cannot generate truly random numbers. In a future where data is worthless, only human behavior can create value through the flow of money. But human behavior still needs time to be thoroughly learned by AI and internalized into engineered, coded expressions. The pursuit of the infinite with the finite is ultimately futile. LLM cannot completely eliminate the illusion; it must approach a point where "this is beyond the reach of both AI and human capability" before the market mechanism can price it, and only then can we truly believe in smart contracts. Current smart contracts are far from successful. The DAO fork, the Curve programming language bug, and even Drift multisignature all prove that "humans have ultimate control over code." Moral judgment has no economic value. The reason why the collaborative model in the DeFi field collapsed from DAOs to foundations and "teams" ultimately stems from the real need for contract upgrades and business collaborations. But humans simply cannot write code that is perpetually secure and dynamically upgradable. Remember, it is impossible. If upgrades are never made, Curve's experience shows us that even a technology dependency stack can run into problems. The present determines the past, and the past determines the future. From the Simons Medallion Fund to Numerai running AI strategies, AI is not uncommon in the financial field. Another counterintuitive case is that trading signals can actually help AI evolve. Image caption: AI and DeFi 10 Years

Image caption: AI and DeFi 10 Years

Image source: @zuoyeweb3

AI models are still based on the computer paradigm, state machines that process signals. Without external signals, they lack the ability to simulate the external world. Yang Lekun and Li Feifei's bet on a world model is significant because of this.

However, from the perspective of DeFi, enabling AI to trade autonomously requires that human intentions be learned by the agent through behavior. This highlights the importance of humans to AI; even if the agent replaces human labor, it is still imitating and summarizing human behavior.

Even more so, humans cannot intentionally be random; slight deliberate actions reveal statistical patterns. Randomness is even more apparent in human physiological characteristics. For example, "I physiologically prefer Ethena's market-making strategy and dislike XX's arbitrage strategy," which carries a vague preference. It's quite certain that making blockchain/DeFi the infrastructure for AI has suffered a dismal failure over the past decade; deAI/deAgent/deOpenclaw will face similar fates. Directly using the latest large-scale models to revamp DeFi's various structures—for example, contracts tested by Mythos have default security, and any modifications are detected in real time, thus increasing the risk level—is a different approach. Regarding human organization, AI's choice is "no humans needed," only human "capabilities." DeFi is the most suitable industry for this, bar none. After rule design, DeFi will only improve capital efficiency under the premise of security. Referring to the L1/2/3/4 levels of autonomous driving, it will inevitably go through the process of information authorization → limited fund usage rights → full fund usage rights. If the Agent continuously learns engineered trader capabilities and Curator management skills, it will inevitably surpass humans in trading and profit. Unfortunately, the accumulated DeFi data has not yet been systematically learned and trained by AI; currently, AI in the crypto space is still in the fundraising stage. However, I am very certain that the actual use of funds will be the next major wave of AI's transformation of DeFi, and this is inevitable. So, what form will token economics take after security (contracts) and organization (humans) are upgraded? Tokens in the PoW era are certificates of computing power consumption, essentially the same as current AI tokens; Tokens in the PoS era are certificates of discounted expected returns, and AI tokens are evolving in this direction (providing the ability to replace humans is the AI ​​expression of this economic value); Crypto tokens in the AI ​​era have already transcended our engineering scope, and we can only make irresponsible predictions based on theory. Referring to Sky using token allocation to control APY across channels, and Claude using token consumption to price model capabilities, future crypto tokens will most likely be certificates of "return on capital." Note the distinction here: Tokens in the PoS era, such as $ETH, have expected returns based on economic assumptions and prior empirical reasoning. However, with the engineering design of AI, DeFi's parameters will infinitely approach reality, making its returns and risk rates highly reliable and verifiable in real time. Users can even determine the current price of a token based on the large model and agents used by the DeFi protocol, as well as the Harness optimization metric score—buying if bullish and selling if bearish. Conclusion: Countless unspeakable anxieties and an unpredictable future for humanity. The future of DeFi can be divided into economic and technological aspects. While there are currently no good solutions for token economics, security offers a glimmer of hope. Claude Mythos can threaten the world; conversely, this means money can be managed effectively. AlphaGo completely solved the Go problem, and Claude completely solved the programming problem. Such scenarios will only increase in the future. DeFi contracts, human organizations, and even economic units of account all have theoretical potential for optimization. At least, people don't need to worry about being completely replaced. In an era where data is worthless, behavior still has its meaning. At least for now, the takeover of people by agents is still limited to details like "micro-tasks" and "micro-payments"—repeated details. We need to make these repetitive, copied behaviors valuable. AI is causing the value of data and content to decrease infinitely, approaching zero cost, while the unit economic value (cost) of AI tokens and crypto tokens is constantly decreasing—this is an inevitable trend. It could even be said that this is the first time money has truly opened its doors to individuals, whether for AI work or for crypto consumption.

data.title: RWA doesn't lack assets, it lacks buyers.
data.description:
Author: JeanW Source: X, @JeanW0708

When analyzing Hong Kong market cases, a characteristic was found: The product structure was complete, and the compliance path was followed, but there were no genuine external buyers.

For example, RWA STHK, with a total size of HK$17.6 million, after reviewing the announcements, it was found that more than half was purchased by the chairman's own BVI company, and the placement was completed four days before the end of the fiscal year; RWA was used for the company's financial statements. Delin Holdings' property tokenization included a HK$60 million allocation in the announcements from the project's inception, essentially "gifting" its own assets to target users.

This illustrates that the problem is not in the execution, but in the asset selection: The tokenization of some assets will not create any value that traditional finance cannot provide, and the motivation for some is not even related to tokenization itself.

Therefore, it's necessary to establish an asset evaluation framework, even a simple one. Question 1: Is this asset worth tokenizing? If not tokenized, will the demand for this asset be met? **Put the asset on two axes:** Horizontal axis: Access friction in traditional finance. This includes geographical restrictions, legal exclusion, minimum investment amounts, and trading time windows. The greater the friction, the higher the "pipeline value" of tokenization. Vertical axis: Real demand density. How many people want exposure to this asset? Is the demand structural or speculative?

Four Quadrants:

High Demand + High Friction → Where RWA Should Be Exist.Tokenized US Treasury bonds are currently the most successful case. A large number of institutions and individuals worldwide need risk-free return exposure, but different investor groups face different types of friction: non-US investors lack a legal channel to directly purchase T-Bills, on-chain protocols and DAO treasuries lack native risk-free return assets, and even US accredited investors face efficiency friction due to slow settlement and lack of composability in traditional funds.

Ondo's USDY opens up US Treasury yield exposure to non-US users through Reg S, resolving access friction; OUGG, based on Reg D, targets US accredited investors, resolving efficiency friction. Different products, different compliance paths, and different frictions combine to support a scale of tens of billions.

Ondo's USDY opens up US Treasury yield exposure to non-US users through Reg S, resolving access friction; OUGG, based on Reg D, targets US accredited investors, resolving efficiency friction.

Different products, different compliance paths, and different frictions, combined, support a scale of tens of billions.

Pre-IPO > US Stocks > US Treasuries. Tokenized gold benefits from physical storage and liquidity friction, independent of policy windows. Pre-IPO equity benefits from structural friction in the PE/VC exit cycle; as long as the holding period in the primary market doesn't shorten, the gaps won't disappear. Tokenized US stocks benefit from global entry barriers; global regulators have significant disagreements regarding security-type RWAs. Tokenized US Treasuries benefit from interest rate cycles; the on-chain world lacks a window of risk-free returns, and a 200 basis point rate cut eliminates the interest rate spread, rendering the product logic worthless.

data.title: A "double-edged sword" interest rate cut strategy? Will Warsh's move give Trump what he wants?
data.description: When Trump chose Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve Chairman, the market widely believed that Warsh must have somehow convinced the president that he could persuade other Fed policymakers to agree to lower interest rates. But the question is, how? For months, one fact has become very clear: among the Fed's 19 top policymakers, few support rate cuts. Fed Governor Milan, who is closest to the White House, has been the "lone voice" advocating for significant rate cuts. But economists say Warsh may be devising a path to get Fed officials to agree to loosen monetary policy: shrinking the Fed's balance sheet. Warsh's previous speeches indicate that he believes shrinking the $1 trillion balance sheet would have the same impact on the economy as a 50 basis point rate hike. Joe Gagnon, a researcher at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, said Warsh would push his Federal Reserve colleagues to lower short-term interest rates to offset the tightening effect of this balance sheet contraction. Essentially, Warsh would tighten policy with one hand and loosen it with the other. But doing so would indeed bring interest rates down. "Reducing the balance sheet may indeed be an argument Warsh uses to justify 'interest rates should be lower,'" said Mark Cabana, head of U.S. interest rate strategy at Bank of America Global Research. Warsh has been an outspoken critic of the size of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet, which has ballooned from less than $1 trillion before the 2008 financial crisis to more than $6 trillion today. The Federal Reserve currently holds $1.6 trillion in long-term Treasury securities and $1.9 trillion in mortgage-backed securities. This is a huge "footprint" in the financial markets. "If you hold mortgage-backed securities and ultra-long-term Treasury securities, then you are pushing down their yields relative to where these assets should be," Gagnon said. Trump's key ally at the Fed, Milan, recently publicly expressed support for this policy. In a speech calling for the Fed to shrink its balance sheet, Milan stated that the economic impact of balance sheet reduction "can be offset by lowering the federal funds rate." "Therefore, resuming balance sheet reduction will likely require further reductions in the federal funds rate beyond the baseline forecast," he said. Gagnon previously served as a senior official at the Federal Reserve before joining the Peterson Institute. He indicated that in the process of lobbying to become Federal Reserve Chairman, Warsh may have told Trump that he sees a path to lower the federal funds rate by 100 basis points. About 50 basis points of this cut would come in response to the balance sheet contraction, and Warsh might argue that another 50 basis points could be implemented after the higher inflation caused by tariffs and the Iran war subsides. Warsh will face questioning on Tuesday regarding his views on interest rates, when the Senate Banking Committee will hold hearings to consider his nomination as Fed Chairman. Lawmakers will focus on whether Warsh will truly remain independent or simply act according to Trump's wishes. Warsh's spokesperson declined to comment. Warsh will face pressure to cut rates. While significant hurdles remain for Warsh's Senate confirmation, whenever he ultimately takes the helm of the Fed, he will face immense pressure from the White House to lower interest rates. Last week, Trump reiterated his stance that interest rates should be well below the current level of 3.5% to 3.75%. In an interview with Fox Business Network, when asked if he thought interest rates would fall later this year, Trump replied, "I think so when Kevin takes office." He has joked that he would sue Warsh if the Federal Reserve doesn't cut rates significantly. However, many Federal Reserve officials say they are cautious about cutting rates until inflation makes progress toward the 2% target. The war with Iran has pushed inflation to its highest level in two years, making officials even more cautious. Ben Bernanke pioneered the use of the balance sheet during his tenure as Federal Reserve chairman in 2008. At the height of the financial crisis, the Fed lowered interest rates to zero, but this was still insufficient to stimulate the economy. So the Federal Reserve purchased long-term Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities to help lower interest rates and boost the economic recovery. In recent years, the Fed has twice attempted to shrink its balance sheet. The first attempt ended in the 2019 market crash, with money market rates soaring. The second attempt began in 2022 and ended relatively quietly last December, but the balance sheet still stands at $6.6 trillion, even higher than its 2017 peak. Gagnon stated that Powell and his colleagues have never figured out a good way to communicate overall monetary policy, including the balance sheet. Fed officials tend to relegate the balance sheet program to a secondary position. They often compare it to "watching the paint dry." "The way they talk about it is as if the balance sheet is one thing and setting interest rates is another; they don't talk about the connection between the two at all," Gagnon said. "What's on the balance sheet does influence policy stance, and they should incorporate it into their overall analysis, but they refuse to do so," he added. Since using a balance sheet in 2008, the Federal Reserve has never sold any assets. Instead, it shrinks its balance sheet by waiting for its holdings to mature and then not reinvesting the proceeds. This strategy has left the Fed with a large amount of mortgage-backed securities. But this strategy of slowly exiting assets upon maturity doesn't work the same way for mortgage-backed securities, because most of them are low-interest loans that homeowners are unlikely to prepay, meaning they may not mature for decades. Warsh's Two Paths to Shrinking the Balance Sheet Bank of America's Cabana stated that the Fed has two options for shrinking its balance sheet. First, it can announce the sale of assets in the open market. He stated that the likelihood of this happening is extremely low. "Selling assets would be incredibly disruptive to financial markets. It would tighten financial conditions and cause a significant decline in the value of risky assets," he said. While an economic slowdown might potentially justify interest rate cuts, the cost would be too high. "I don't think Trump would agree to that at all," Cabana said. In a speech last month, Federal Reserve Governor Milan stated that he would support selling certain securities if they were profitable. But Cabana said he believes other Fed officials would not agree. "If the Fed tries to cash in profits, the market will be very surprised. Most experts will tell you it's highly unlikely. I would also tell you it's highly unlikely," Cabana said. He stated that a more viable option is for the Federal Reserve to focus on the liability side of its balance sheet—the $3 trillion in reserves held by banks at the Fed. Banks hold these reserves in part to meet liquidity standards established after the 2008 financial crisis. Darrell Duffie, a finance professor at Stanford University's Graduate School of Business, stated that reserves are a useful form of liquidity for banks. They are ultra-short-term and highly liquid assets. "This can help banks weather liquidity crises when they desperately need funds," he said on a Brookings Institution podcast. The Fed wants banks to hold ample reserves. At the same time, Duffie noted that the Fed is sensitive to the amount of interest it pays to commercial banks on deposits, as this is often portrayed as "giving away money" to banks. "At least for some people—including some in Congress—it doesn't look very respectable that large banks are getting a lot of interest from the Fed," Duffy said. But he pointed out that there's actually no real cost, as banks can earn the same amount of interest by holding short-term Treasury bills. Cleveland Fed President Hammarck told CNBC last Wednesday that the Fed is struggling to balance the financial stability benefits of ample reserves with some reputational benefits of shrinking its balance sheet. Fed Governor Waller put it more vividly in a February speech: "You don't want banks to be rummaging through their sofa cushions every night looking for money. That's incredibly inefficient and stupid." But there are limits to reducing reserve requirements. "If banks continue to demand reserves, we can only shrink our balance sheet to a certain extent," Hammarck said. Lou Crandall, chief economist at Wrightson ICAP, a research firm specializing in Federal Reserve studies, said central banks are making progress in exploring possible options for reducing banks' excessive reserves. There has been discussion about reforming the liquidity rules implemented after 2008—rules that incentivize banks to hold reserves while establishing programs to ensure banks have access to emergency funding when needed. Duffy stated that another, more controversial idea is for the Federal Reserve to pay lower interest rates on bank reserves exceeding a certain threshold. "If we can find a way to make the banking industry believe it doesn't need to hoard so many reserves, then the Federal Reserve can shrink its balance sheet…it's a win-win for everyone," Crandall said. Bank of America's Cabana stated that if the Federal Reserve can manage to reduce its need for reserves, it will likely stick to its plan to shrink its balance sheet by allowing assets to mature. Guy LeBas, chief fixed-income strategist at Janney Capital Management, said that it took 20 years to develop these complex regulations and policies that require large reserves, and changes to these regulations are "difficult to predict all possible outcomes." "I doubt these policies will change anytime soon," he said. Balance sheet shrinking does not necessarily mean lower interest rates. While Warsh might argue that shrinking the balance sheet should lead to lower interest rates, some experts are skeptical. "While the argument for a 100-basis-point rate cut is broadly plausible, it's far from a done deal," Jon Faust, a former senior advisor to Powell and now a professor of economics at Johns Hopkins University, said in an email. "Creating a story about a rate cut for a Trump eager to do so is a completely different matter from convincing a skeptical FOMC," he added. The FOMC is the body responsible for setting interest rates. Even if it were possible, reforming bank regulation would take a long time. This is unlikely to satisfy an impatient president. "I doubt the federal funds rate will come down before the November midterm elections," Faust said. Bank of America's Cabana stated that he ultimately believes the market will not accept Warsh's argument that a balance sheet reduction achieved in a way that does not disrupt the financial environment justifies interest rate cuts. "That's likely the kind of reasoning they'll use. But the market will be highly skeptical of that argument. If you decide to cut rates, that increases the risk of inflation," he said.
